zh.Englefield, Berkshire, England: 英國英格蘭波克夏恩格利費爾德

zh-hans.Englefield, Berkshire, England: 英国英格兰波克夏恩格利費爾德

nameMeanings.name: Young
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English, Scottish, and northern Irish: nickname from Middle English yong ‘young’ (Old English geong), used to distinguish a younger man from an older man bearing the same personal name (typically, father and son). In Middle English this name is often found with the Anglo-Norman French definite article, for example Robert le Yunge. In Gaelic-speaking areas of Scotland this was widely used as an English equivalent of the Gaelic nickname Og ‘young’; see Ogg . This surname is also very common among African Americans.

Americanized form (translation into English) of various European surnames meaning ‘young’ or similar, notably German   Jung  , Dutch   Jong   and   De Jong  , and French   Lejeune   and   Lajeunesse  .
Americanized form of Swedish Ljung: topographic or an ornamental name from ljung ‘(field of) heather’, or a habitational name from a placename containing this word, e.g. Ljungby.

variants.name: Yongue
meanings: English: variant of   Young  . This form of the surname is now rare in Britain.
Perhaps also an Americanized form of Dutch   De Jonge  .

variants.name: Youngs
meanings: English (Norfolk): variant of Young , with post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: Younger
meanings: English (mainly Northumberland and eastern Scotland): from Middle English yungre ‘younger’, comparative of Young , used to distinguish a younger man from an older man bearing the same personal name (typically, father and son). In one case, at least, however, the name is known to have been borne by an immigrant Fleming, and was probably an Americanized form of Middle Dutch jongheer ‘young nobleman’ (see Jonker ).
Americanized form of various cognate or like-sounding names in other languages, notably German   Junger   and   Junker  , or Dutch   Jonker  .

variants.name: Ogg
meanings: Scottish: Anglicized form of a nickname from the Gaelic adjective óg ‘young’, used to distinguish the junior of two bearers of the same personal name (typically, father and son).
English (Lincolnshire): variant of   Hogg  , pronounced without initial /h/.

variants.name: Younge
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Fitzroy, Sinead.

English: variant of   Young  . — Note: The population figure published by the US Census Bureau for this surname in the year 2000 is as high as 823, while the figure for 2010 is supposedly lower than 100.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

nameMeanings.name: Young,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: ‘Brave’.

nameMeanings.name: Bishop
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from Middle English bissop, biscop, Old English bisc(e)op ‘bishop’, which comes via Latin from Greek episkopos ‘overseer’. The Greek word was adopted early in the Christian era as a title for an overseer of a local community of Christians, and has yielded cognates in every European language: French évêque, Italian vescovo, Spanish obispo, Russian yepiskop, German Bischof, etc. The word came to be applied as a surname for a variety of reasons, among them a supposed resemblance in bearing or appearance to a bishop, and selection as the ‘boy bishop’ on Saint Nicholas's Feast Day. In some instances the surname is from the rare Middle English (Old English) personal name Biscop ‘bishop’. As an Irish surname it is adopted for Mac Giolla Easpaig, meaning ‘servant of the bishop’ (see Gillespie ). In North America, this surname has absorbed, by assimilation and translation, at least some of continental European cognates, e.g. German Bischoff , Polish, Rusyn, Czech, and Slovak Biskup , Slovenian Škof (see Skoff ).

variants.name: Bischoff
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Otto, Erwin, Bernhard, Hans, Klaus, Lorenz, Alois, Armin, Arno, Dieter, Fritz.

German: from Middle High German bischof, Middle Low German bischop ‘bishop’, probably applied as a nickname for someone with a pompous manner or as a metonymic occupational name for someone in the service of a bishop. See also Bishop .

variants.name: Bispo
meanings: Portuguese and Galician: nickname from bispo ‘bishop’ (see Bishop ).

variants.name: Gillespie
meanings: Anglicized form of Gaelic Gille Easbaig ‘bishop's servant’. Gille Easbaig Cambeul was the founder of Clan Campbell.

variants.name: Episcopo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Orazio, Pasquale, Rocco.

Italian: from episcopo ‘bishop’ (from Latin episcopus (see Bishop ), probably a nickname for someone who had a pious manner or perhaps a metonymic occupational name for someone in the service of a bishop).

variants.name: Dean
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, which has a double origin. In part it is a local name for someone who lived in a valley (Middle English dene, Old English denu) and in part an occupational name for someone who served as a dean, i.e. ecclesiastical supervisor (Latin decanus). The given name also sometimes represents Italian Dino (a short form of names such as Bernardino), as in the case of the American actor and singer Dean Martin ( 1917–95 ).

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: When Richard Young, Bishop Young was born in 1375, in England, his father, Sir Thomas Young, was 30 and his mother, Beatrice Kenton, was 30. He married Alice Rossell about 1408, in England. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died in 1451, in his hometown, at the age of 76.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard Young, Bishop Young est né en 1375, à Angleterre, son père, Sir Thomas Young, avait 30 ans et sa mère, Beatrice Kenton, avait 30 ans. Il a épousé Alice Rossell environ 1408, à Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé en 1451, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 76 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als Richard Young, Bishop Young 1375 in England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Sir Thomas Young 30 und seine Mutter Beatrice Kenton 30. Er hat um 1408 in England Alice Rossell geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1451 im Alter von 76 Jahren in seinem Geburtsort.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Richard Young, Bishop Young è nato il nel 1375 nel England, il suo padre, Sir Thomas Young, aveva 30 anni e il suo madre, Beatrice Kenton, aveva 30 anni. Lui sposato Alice Rossell circa 1408, nel England. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio. Morì nel 1451, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 76 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: Richard Young, Bishop Young(이)가 1375년 영국에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir Thomas Young(은)는 30세였고, 그의 어머니 Beatrice Kenton(은)는 30세였다. 그는 대략 1408년 영국에서 Alice Rossell(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1451년 그의 고향에서 76세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Richard Young, Bishop Young nasceu em 1375, em Inglaterra, seu pai, Sir Thomas Young, tinha 30 anos e sua mãe, Beatrice Kenton, tinha 30 anos. Ele casou-se com Alice Rossell aproximadamente 1408, em Inglaterra. Eles tiveram pelo menos 1 filho. Ele faleceu em 1451, em sua cidade natal, com 76 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Richard Young, Bishop Young родился в 1375 г. в местности Англия, его отцу по имени Sir Thomas Young было 30 лет, а матери, которую звали Beatrice Kenton, 30. Он женился на женщине по имени Alice Rossell около 1408 г. в местности Англия. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын. Он умер в 1451 г. в его родном городе в возрасте 76 лет.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Richard Young, Bishop Young nació en 1375, en Inglaterra, su papá, Sir Thomas Young, tenía 30 años y su mamá, Beatrice Kenton, tenía 30 años. Se casó con Alice Rossell aproximadamente 1408, en Inglaterra. Fueron padres de por lo menos 1 hijo. Murió en 1451, en su pueblo, a la edad de 76 años.
